coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot-decorated metal-organic framework composite for Fe3+ detection in the aqueous environment
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Shixin
Li, Wei
Zheng, Mingtao
Zheng, Yinjian
Li, Qingming
Liang, Jiarong
Chen, Jianying
Huang, Xiaoman
Liu, Yingliang
Zhang, Xuejie
Lei, Bingfu
Zhang, Haoran
coredata.dc:description: The contamination of heavy metal ions in the aqueous environment constitutes an important threat to human survival. Hence, the development of simple, effective, and cheap detection of heavy metals and pesticide residues is essential for environmental control. Here, we synthesized a yellow carbon dots-linked ZIF-8 (yCDs/ZIF-8) composite, which was prepared with excellent photoluminescence properties and aqueous stability, and characterized by strong yellow fluorescence emission. The ratio metric fluorescence sensor has been constructed using its stable light blue carbon dots. The fluorescence sensor has excellent selectivity and sensitivity as a sensing probe for Fe3+ ions, with a detection limit of 0.3690 μM. In addition, the fluorescence quench mechanism was investigated. It has been shown that the yCDs/ZIF-8 fluorescence could be quenched by Fe3+ via static quenching and absorption competition mechanisms. The probe has high-quality application potential in terms of food safety and environmental monitoring due to the fact of its simplicity, high yield, and low cost.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Metal-organic frameworks
Ratiometric fluorescent probes
Fe3+ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Construction of specific fluorescent probes and detection of Fe3+
Detection of Fe3+ in real samples
Application of yCDs/ZIF-8 in actual water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic strategy of yCDs, ZIF-8, and yCDs/ZIF-8.

(a,b) SEM images of (a) ZIF-8. (b) yCDs/ZIF-8. (c) TEM images of yCDs. (d) HRTEM image of yCDs/ZIF-8. (e) XRD patterns of yCDs, ZIF-8, and yCDs/ZIF-8. (f) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of ZIF-8, yCDs/ZIF-8.
(a) FTIR spectra of yCDs, ZIF-8, and yCDs/ZIF-8. (b) XPS spectra of yCDs. (c–e) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s of yCDs.
High-resolution XPS spectra of ZIF-8 and yCDs/ZIF-8 for C 1s (a), N 1s (b), O 1s (c), and Zn 2p (d).

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum, excitation spectrum, and emission spectrum of yCDs. (The inset shows images of yCDs solution in sunlight and under UV light). (b) UV–Vis absorption spectrum, excitation spectrum, and emission spectrum of yCDs/ZIF-8 composites. (c) Normalized fluorescence emission spectra of yCDs, ZIF-8, and yCDs/ZIF-8. (d) TGA and DTG curves of yCDs/ZIF-8.

(a) Emission spectrums at various excitation wavelengths. (b) Comparison of fluorescence intensities of yCDs/ZIF-8 solution after the addition of various metal ions (different metal ions with a concentration of 10−2 M, λex = 385 nm). (c) Fluorescence spectra of yCDs/ZIF-8 system with various concentrations of Fe3+ (λex = 380 nm). (d) A linear curve for the detection of Fe3+ for the sensor. The concentration of Fe3+ was between 10 and 80 μM, and the concentration of Fe3+ was linear with I465/I540.
Generation cycles of fluorescence intensity after Fe3+ detection by yCDs/ZIF-8.
(a) PXRD images and (b) FT-IR spectra of yCDs/ZIF-8 in the absence and presence of 1 × 10−2 mol/L Fe3+.

(a) Time-resolved decay curves of yCDs/ZIF-8 within the existence and nonexistence (black and red curves) of 1 × 10−2 mol/L Fe3+. (b) Superpose between the absorption band of Fe3+ and the excitation band of the yCDs/ZIF-8. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of yCDs/ZIF-8, Fe3+, and yCDs/ZIF-8-Fe3+ system, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Comparison of diverse materials for Fe3+ detection.
Determination of Fe3+ in actual samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot-decorated metal-organic framework composite for Fe3+ detection in the aqueous environment

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Maoming Branch, Guangdong Laboratory for Lingnan Modern Agriculture, Guangdong Maoming, 525100, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Laboratory for Lingnan Modern Agriculture

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The contamination of heavy metal ions in the aqueous environment constitutes an important threat to human survival. Hence, the development of simple, effective, and cheap detection of heavy metals and pesticide residues is essential for environmental control. Here, we synthesized a yellow carbon dots-linked ZIF-8 (yCDs/ZIF-8) composite, which was prepared with excellent photoluminescence properties and aqueous stability, and characterized by strong yellow fluorescence emission. The ratio metric fluorescence sensor has been constructed using its stable light blue carbon dots. The fluorescence sensor has excellent selectivity and sensitivity as a sensing probe for Fe3+ ions, with a detection limit of 0.3690 μM. In addition, the fluorescence quench mechanism was investigated. It has been shown that the yCDs/ZIF-8 fluorescence could be quenched by Fe3+ via static quenching and absorption competition mechanisms. The probe has high-quality application potential in terms of food safety and environmental monitoring due to the fact of its simplicity, high yield, and low cost.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a kind of new type of porous crystalline materials and have been widely used in many fields such as sensing [1–4], catalytic [5–8], gas storage [9], adsorption [10], separation [11], drug delivery [12], and bioimaging owing to their outstanding physicochemical properties, which have unique pore structure, adjustable pore size, large specific surface area, etc. As a microporous sensing material, MOFs, such as zeolitic imidazole skeleton-8 [13], MIL-101 [14], and UiO-66 [15] have also been explored as fluorescence quenching agents in biosensor research. However, these MOFs exhibit insufficient fluorescence quenching efficiency (QE), long quenching times, and high detection limits [16]. It is worth noting that MOF-based composites may combine the detection properties of MOF with another precursor to enhance their detection performance. As a result, MOF-based composites are expected to be developed as highly selective fluorescence sensors and high fluorescence quenching efficiencies for targeted materials.

As early as 2004, Zhao et al. [17] explored metal-organic framework-based (MOF-based) heavy metal ion sensors, followed by numerous biosensors used to detect heavy metal ions. For instance, Liu et al. [18] proposed Zn-MOF for Fe3+ detection with a detection limit of 0.89 μM; Zhang et al. [19] have developed a two-step approach to encapsulating perovskite quantum dots into a MOF-5 matrix as stable fluorescence probes for Al3+, Bi3+, Co2+, Cu2+, Cd2+ in aqueous solution ((2–10) × 10−3 M) with low detection limits; Yao et al. [15] proposed CDs@Uio-66(OH)2 materials for Fe3+ detection and its detection limit of 0.76 μM. Compared to other composite precursors such as metal nanoparticles, perovskite, etc. Carbon dots (CDs) as a new kind of carbon nanomaterial with abundant organic functional groups, superior optical properties, excellent water solubility, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility [4,20,21], resulting in the potential to be widely used as an ion sensor [22]. From these, CDs and MOFs composites hold the promise of a new class of fluorescence sensors for further development. In addition, CDs and MOFs materials can be constructed into ratio metric fluorescence (RF) sensors [1], eliminating possible variations in instrument characteristics and the intensity of unstable light sources, improving their selectivity, sensitivity, and reducing detection limits.

With the development of industry, heavy metal ion pollution in the aquatic environments has become a major concern, threatening the security of human existence. Iron is a transition metal and a vital part of life. However, the increase of Fe3+ ions in water resources poses an outstanding threat to human health. Contaminated water can cause acute or chronic toxicity to humans and can cause many infectious diseases [23–25]. Thus, the detection of Fe3+ in the water environment is of great significance. In this work, we synthesized a fluorescent composite of yCDs/ZIF-8 with ultra-high sensitivity using [3-(2-aminoethylamino)propyl]trimethoxysilane (AEATMS) as a linker. An ultra-high selectivity and sensitivity proportional fluorescent probe was constructed with extremely stable BCDs to detect Fe3+. The proposed probe exhibits two separate emission peaks at 465 nm and 540 nm, with the fluorescence at 540 nm decreasing with increasing Fe3+ concentration and the fluorescence at 465 nm increasing with increasing Fe3+ concentration. Apart from this, yCDs/ZIF-8 also has excellent water solubility and stability, which provides more favorable conditions for the detection of heavy metal ions in the environment.

Succinic anhydride (98%, AR; C4H4O3), o-phenylenediamine (98%, AR; C6H4(NH2)2), citric acid (99.5%, AR; C6H8O7), 2-methylimidazole (98%, AR; C4H6N2), N-aminoethyl-γ-aminopropyl-trimethoxysilane (95%, AR; C8H22N2O3Si) were purchased from Macklin Corporation. Anhydrous ethanol (99.5%, AR; C2H6O), dichloromethane (99.5%, AR; CH2Cl2), methanol (99.5%, AR; CH3OH) were purchased from Shanghai Titan Chemical Co. Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (99.0%, AR; Zn(NO3)2·6H2O) and trimethylolaminomethane (99.9%, AR; C4H11NO3) were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory, and the above chemical reagents have been used with no other purification.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) experiments were carried out using a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope and a JEOL-2010 electron microscope. UV–visible absorption spectra were collected by a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV–visible spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra had been recorded with the usage of a Hitachi F7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer and an Edinburgh Instruments FLS1000 instrument. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were acquired using a Vertex 70 FT-IR spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained by an x-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo ESCALAB 250XI). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was taken from a Zeiss scanning electron microscope (ULTRA Plus).

The y-CDs were prepared using the classical solvothermal method. Briefly, 0.2500 g of succinic anhydride and 0.1080 g of o-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 5 mL of water. Then, the mixed solution was placed into a reaction kettle lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. When cooling to ambient temperature, the solvent was removed by evaporation, eluted with a combination of dichloromethane and methyl alcohol (dichloromethane:methanol = 12:1) and purified by chromatography. After purification, a yellow-green carbon spot solution is obtained, which is eventually dispersed in ethanol by spin evaporation.

The citric acid (1.921 g) and trimethylolaminomethane (1.211 g) (molar ratio 1:1) were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water and heated in a toaster oven on medium-high power for 6 min, then cooled to acquire a yellow-brown solid, which was dissolved in water and set aside.

The synthesis of ZIF-8 followed the method previously reported in the literature [26]. Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (2.503 g) was dissolved in 10 mL methanol to form the A solution, and 2-methylimidazole (2.675 g) was dissolved in 10 mL methanol to form the B solution. A and B were mixed and sent to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 120 °C for 6 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and flooded with methanol on three occasions. Finally, the samples were dried in an oven at 80 °C for 12 h.

The yCDs/ZIF-8 was synthesized using the approach previously depicted in the literature [27]. 0.1 g ZIF-8 was mixed with 1.0 mL AEATMS at room temperature, 1.0 mL yCDs (0.0215 mg/mL) were added and stirred for 1 h. The sample was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min and dried at 80 °C for 12 h to gain a yellow sample powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of specific fluorescent probes and detection of Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: All the following reactions and measurements were performed at ambient temperature, and the fluorescence emission spectra were aggregated at 380 nm excitation to construct a fluorescence detection system. Briefly, 2.0 mL (5.0 mg/mL) of the composite was mixed with 10 μL (3.0 mg/mL) of a solution of BCDs and poured into the groove of the cuvette, then different concentrations of Fe3+ solutions were added to the colorimetric tube bath and their emission spectra were collected at 380 nm excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The environmental water samples applied in the experiment were derived from the South China Agricultural University Wetland Park, Guangzhou, Guangdong Province. All water samples are centrifuged in a centrifuge prior to analysis, then filtered and used by filtration of a membrane of 0.22 μm. Standard sample addition methods are then applied to verify the suitability of the sensor.

The synthesis of yCDs and ZIF-8 was distributed by a facile solvothermal methodology. AEATMS was used as linkers to synthesize yCDs/ZIF-8 and a synthesis strategy schematic is illustrated in Fig. 1 . Since yCDs and ZIF-8 are rich in functional groups like amino and carboxyl groups, AEATMS have amino groups as well as Si–O groups, supra-molecular interactions (by means of hydrogen connections or other types of hydrophilic interactions) can occur between yCDs, ZIF-8, and AEATMS [27], leading to the formation of yCDs/ZIF-8. To further demonstrate that yCDs/ZIF-8 was linked by chemical bonding instead of electrostatic interactions, we first investigated the surface charges of yCDs and ZIF-8 by mensuration of potentials. As represented in Fig. S1, yCDs had a potential of 16 mV whereas ZIF-8 had a potential of 48 mV. Consequently, the synthesis of yCDs and ZIF-8 is not connected by electrostatic interactions but by chemical bonds [2,9,28]. The formation of those chemical bonds is verified in the following discussion of IR and XPS.

To investigate the morphology and structural characteristics, SEM, TEM, and HRTEM were used to analyse ZIF-8, yCDs/ZIF-8. As exhibited in Fig. 2 a, ZIF-8 is a regular rhombic dodecahedra and rhombohedra cubic crystal structure, which is consistent with the reported literature [13]. Correspondingly, after yCDs are doped into ZIF-8, yCDs/ZIF-8 also show regular rhombohedra dodecahedra in Fig. 2b, only the surface becomes relatively rough. Fig. 2c exhibits a TEM image of the yCDs, from which it can be seen that the yCDs are inhomogeneous spheres with an average diameter of 2.53 nm. TEM displays an apparent lattice (inset) with lattice stripe spacing of 0.15 nm, indicating that the yCDs are highly crystalline. In addition, we observed lattice stripes of yCDs in HRTEM images of yCDs/ZIF-8 (Fig. 2d). These results indicate that we have successfully synthesized yCDs/ZIF-8.

As depicted in Fig. 2e, the XRD patterns exhibit distinctive diffraction peaks at (011), (002), (112), (022), (013), and (222) that are similar to those mentioned in the literature [13]. This suggests that it is possible to synthesize ZIF-8 using this approach. The diffraction peak position of the yCDs/ZIF-8 composite material is exactly consistent with the diffraction peak position of ZIF-8, indicating that yCDs/ZIF-8 maintains crystal integrity after loading yCDs and ZIF-8 with AEATMS. Furthermore, the adsorption-desorption isotherm curve of N2 of ZIF-8 belongs to type I isotherm (Fig. 2f) and shows a sudden rise in the low-pressure region [29], which demonstrates that the synthesized ZIF-8 is dominated by microporous structure with an aperture around 1.1 nm (Inset). The BET surface area of yCDs/ZIF-8 in the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm (Fig. 2f) was 576 m2/g, much lower than the 646 m2/g of ZIF-8. This is further evidence of the successful complexation of carbon dots with ZIF-8.

To demonstrate the presence of chemical composition and various functional groups in yCDs, ZIF-8, yCDs/ZIF-8, the functional groups of the surface of yCDs, ZIF-8, yCDs/ZIF-8 were studied by FT-IR and XPS. As exhibited in Fig. 3 a, the yCDs, ZIF-8, and yCDs/ZIF-8 are all rich in polar functional groups. yCDs have the stretching vibrations of C–H (3680 cm−1), C–OH (3207 cm−1), C–C (2910 cm−1), CO (1735 cm−1), C–N (1068 cm−1), respectively [21,30–35]. The peaks of ZIF-8 are situated at 3136, 2923, 1568, 1143, and 993 cm−1, while 3136 cm−1 and 2923 cm−1 were associated with C–H stretching vibrations attributed to imidazole and methyl groups, respectively. 1568 cm−1 apply to CN stretching vibrations in imidazole, 1143 cm−1 and 993 cm−1 correspond to the C–N stretching vibration [34,35]. When yCDs are compounded with ZIF-8, the C–N and N–H bonds were significantly enhanced and new bonds (amide bond formation at 1411 cm−1) were present [36–38], and the formation of amide bonds was further evidence that yCDs/ZIF-8 was linked by chemical bonds rather than electrostatic interactions.

The XPS spectra (Fig. 3b) show that yCDs principally consisted of carbon (C, 89.29%), nitrogen (N, 4.19%), and oxygen (O, 6.52%), with carbon (C 1s), nitrogen (N 1s) and oxygen (O 1s) present at 284.8 eV, 399.5 eV and 531.5 eV binding energy. For yCDs, the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) can be deconvoluted into three peaks located at 284.5 eV, 284.9 eV, and 285.3 eV, which can be assigned to C–C, CC/C–C, C–N, respectively [15,32]. 399.3 eV and 399.8 eV in the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) correspond to amino nitrogen and CN [39]. The high-resolution spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 3e) consists of two peaks with a binding energy of 531.5 eV and 531.9 eV, corresponding to CO and C–OH, respectively [15,21,31–33], which is consistent with the FTIR characterization. To sum up, yCDs have plentiful amino and carboxyl groups on their surface, ZIF-8 contains N–H bond and AEATMS have a large number of amino groups, which makes it easy to form supra-molecular interactions groups, leading to the formation of yCDs-AEATMS-ZIF-8. Furthermore, as can be seen from Fig. 4 , the binding energy of the C 1s spectrum increases after doping with yCDs, while the O 1s, N 1s and Zn 2p binding energies all decrease, indicating that the yCDs are linked to ZIF-8 due to the action of the organic linker AEATMS [40], which further suggests that yCDs were attached to ZIF-8 by chemical bonding.

To evaluate the fluorescence quenching effect of the fluorescence sensing system, the absorption spectra, and fluorescence spectra were analyzed. The UV absorption spectrum of the yCDs exhibits four absorption peaks (Fig. 5 a). The absorptions peak at 210 nm and 270 nm are attributed to π→π* transition of CC and n→π* transition of CO, respectively [31–33,39], and the absorptions at 243 nm and 382 nm are ascribed to π→π* of CC and C–N, respectively [3,36], [39,41,42]. Therefore, the source of fluorescence of yCDs is primarily the n→π* transition of electrons [43]. In addition to this, the optimal emission of yCDs is 529 nm at the 382 nm optimum excitation. The aqueous solution of yCDs is transparent yellow in the visible light and fluoresces bright yellow-green in 365 nm UV light. In contrast, after compounding with ZIF-8, the luminescence center of the composite was red-shifted from 529 nm to 536 nm (Fig. 5b), which may be owing to the effect of electron leap.

Fig. 5d shows the thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curves of the as-prepared yCDs/ZIF-8 composites, which depicted three main weight loss steps. There is a slight weight loss change in the composites at 60–300 °C. This part of the mass change may be due to the dehydration of the metal-organic framework. The second part is mainly owing to the breaking of the chemical bond between the yCDs and the ZIF-8 surface. When the chemical bond is broken, the carbon dots separate from the ZIF-8, losing their original stability and decomposing rapidly at 600 °C, resulting in a loss of mass [44]. The last part is caused by the collapse of the metal framework of ZIF-8. Above all, yCDs/ZIF-8 has excellent optical properties and thermal stability, which provide potential conditions for the detection of Fe3+.

To construct a proportional fluorescence sensing system, we added blue carbon dots (BCDs) to provide a blue emission center. Build a fluorescence sensing system by fixing the amount of BCDs used, and obtain the optimal excitation wavelength of the fluorescence sensing system at that ratio (Fig. 6 a) [45].

The selectivity of synthesized yCDs/ZIF-8 was investigated by making use of the fluorescence quenching effect of different metal ions (containing Zn2+, Ba2+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, K+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Cu2+, and Fe3+). As expected, the fluorescence of yCDs/ZIF-8 was only quenched by the addition of Fe3+, while other metal ions had a little or non-interference with fluorescence (Fig. 6b). These results proved that the prepared yCDs/ZIF-8 was superb sensitivity towards the Fe3+ ions and can be served as fluorescence sensor probes for the selective detection of Fe3+ ions. In addition, we use the same method for yCDs and BCDs. As shown in Figs. S5a–b, the BCDs solution showed a little decrease in fluorescence after the addition of Fe3+ and the quenching effect can be ignorable. Fig. 6c showed the fluorescence quenching of yCDs/ZIF-8 at different Fe3+ concentrations. The results showed that, with the optimized circumstances, the fluorescence emission intensity of the fluorescent system at 540 nm decreased with increasing Fe3+ concentration, while that at 465 nm increased. Within the concentration range of 10–80 μM, I465/I540 shows higher linearity with Fe3+ with an R2 = 0.9854 correlation coefficient and detection limit of 0.3690 μM (Fig. 6d). The yCDs/ZIF-8 composite has a high sensitivity for Fe3+ detection compared to that reported in the literature (Table 1 ).

Recyclability also plays an essential part in the development of green chemistry. Therefore, the recovery performance of yCDs/ZIF-8 is also considered. As exhibited in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence intensity of the composite material at 536 nm is easily recovered that more than 95% of the original after 4 cycles. Furthermore, the crystalline structure of the material is not destroyed after the cycle (Fig. 8 a-b). These results indicate that yCDs/ZIF-8 has excellent reusability and a certain practical application prospect in the detection of Fe3+ [47].

To further study the quenching mechanism of this fluorescence sensor system, we first investigated the fluorescence lifetime before and after the addition of Fe3+. As depicted in Fig. 9 a, the average decay curve of yCDs/ZIF-8 is 2.55 ns. The average fluorescence lifetime of yCDs/ZIF-8 in the presence of Fe3+ is 2.36 ns. It is noteworthy that the lifetime of yCDs/ZIF-8 did without significant change after the addition of Fe3+. This result shows that the quenching mechanism will be static quenching [50,51]. Second, when static quenching occurs, strong interactions between the quencher and fluorophore form non-fluorescent ground state complexes [49], which cause changes in the absorption spectra of the fluorophore. To further demonstrate this mechanism, we studied the UV absorption spectra of yCDs/ZIF-8 in the absence and presence of Fe3+. As exhibited in Fig. 9c, the absorption peak of yCDs/ZIF-8 at 230 nm is red-shifted in the presence of Fe3+, indicating a strong interaction between yCDs/ZIF-8 and Fe3+ and the formation of the ground state complex. Finally, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of Fe3+ ion partially overlaps with yCDs/ZIF-8 at 300–400 nm (Fig. 9b), indicating that there is also absorption competition for this quenching process [52,53]. To sum up, the identification of yCDs/ZIF-8 for Fe3+ can be attributed to static quenching and absorption competition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of yCDs/ZIF-8 in actual water
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the reliability and accuracy of this fluorescence sensor, Fe3+ was detected in a water sample using a standard addition method [54]. The results of the analysis can be found in Table 2 . The spike recovery of this method was calculated by adding various concentrations of Fe3+ standard solution to the samples. Satisfactory recoveries (92.0%–99.8%) with low relative standard deviation (<5%) were obtained, which demonstrates that fluorescent composites have a strong capacity for application in the Fe3+ ion detection fluorescence probe in the analysis of actual water samples.

In conclusion, we have prepared a new y-CDs/ZIF-8 fluorescent composite material and constructed a ratio metric fluorescence sensor with stable blue carbon dots. Unlike previously developed fluorescent probes, the synthesized yCDs/ZIF-8 probe is highly sensitive and selective for Fe3+ with a detection limit of 0.3690 μM. In addition, the probe had a simple, convenient, and low detection limit, a large linearity range, and excellent selectivity, permitting easy detection of Fe3+ concentrations in the aqueous environment. The recoveries (92.0%–99.8%) and RSD values (1.42%–2.96%) of the actual water samples analysis demonstrated that the method has good potential for biosensing applications.

Shixin Wu: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Wei Li: Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Formal analysis. Mingtao Zheng: Writing – review & editing. Yinjian Zheng: Writing – review & editing. Qingming Li: Writing – review & editing. Jiarong Liang: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Jianying Chen: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Xiaoman Huang: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Yingliang Liu: Writing – review & editing. Xuejie Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Bingfu Lei: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Haoran Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Guangdong Provincial Special Fund for Modern Agriculture Industry Technology Innovation Teams (Nos. 2022KJ122, 2023KJ122), the Independent Research and Development Projects of Maoming Laboratory (No. 2021ZZ004), the National Natural Science Foundations of China (No. 12274144), the Key Realm R&D Program of Guangdong Province (No. 2021B0707010003), the Guangzhou Science & Technology Project (Nos. 202007020005, 202103000059), the Guangdong Provincial Science and Technology Project (Nos. 2021A0505050006, 2022A1515010229), the Project of GDUPS (2018) for Prof. Bingfu LEI, the Ministry of Science and Technology of China (No. G2021030022L), and the Undergraduate Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program grant for Chun Chen (No. X202210564002).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.micromeso.2022.112254.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal–organic frameworks as photoluminescent biosensing platforms: mechanisms and applications
Chem. Soc. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhao, H. Zeng, X.W. Zhu, W. Lu, D. Li, Metal-organic frameworks as photoluminescent biosensing platforms: mechanisms and applications, Chem. Soc. Rev. 50 (2021) 4484-4513.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An efficient ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on metal-organic frameworks and quantum dots for highly selective detection of 6-mercaptopurine
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Jin, Z.L. Mou, R.L. Zhang, S.S. Liang, Z.Q. Zhang, An efficient ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on metal-organic frameworks and quantum dots for highly selective detection of 6-mercaptopurine, Biosens. Bioelectron. 91 (2017) 162-168.
